skill.name: saga-orchestration
skill.description: Implement saga orchestration and choreography patterns for distributed transactions, compensating actions, and long-running backend workflows.
skill.summary: Design resilient saga workflows for distributed transactions and failure recovery.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell execution, network, blocker, weak crypto, and reconnaissance patterns. Manual review found these are false positives from markdown code fences, architecture diagram text, an import in sample code, best-practice prose, and two documentation links. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection, data exfiltration, executable setup logic, or runtime network behavior.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The reported backtick execution locations are markdown code block delimiters around diagrams and Python examples. They do not execute commands or instruct the agent to run shell code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each location is a markdown fence delimiter, not Ruby backtick execution. The surrounding content is explanatory documentation and Python sample code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Documentation Links Flagged as Network Behavior
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are resource links in the markdown Resources section. They are not fetched by code and do not send local data to external services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both URLs appear as plain markdown reference links. No executable network client, request body, credential access, or automatic download behavior is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Blocker Patterns Are Benign Documentation Terms
low_findings.description: The high-severity blocker hits map to architecture diagram labels, descriptive prose, and a standard uuid import in a Python example. No evidence found of command-and-control logic, weak cryptographic implementation, or host reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain markdown metadata, table text, diagram labels, a design advice sentence, a book link, and import uuid. None show malware intent or dangerous behavior in context.

content.value_statement: Distributed workflows can fail after only some services complete their work. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code design saga steps, compensations, timeouts, and event flows.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use orchestration or choreography for saga workflows.
Provides Python templates for base saga orchestrators and order fulfillment flows.
Models compensating transactions for inventory, payment, shipping, and notification steps.
Shows event handlers for saga step success, failure, and compensation.
Includes timeout handling patterns for long-running saga steps.
Lists practical design rules for idempotency, correlation IDs, and partial failures.
limitations: The examples are templates and require adaptation to a real message bus and storage layer.
It does not include production persistence, retry backoff, or monitoring integrations.
It does not verify business rules, payment compliance, or service ownership boundaries.
It focuses on Python-style examples, though the concepts apply across backend stacks.

use_cases.title: Order Fulfillment Design
use_cases.description: Design a multi-step order process with inventory reservation, payment, shipment, notifications, and rollback actions.

use_cases.title: Distributed Failure Recovery
use_cases.description: Plan compensating actions when a long-running workflow fails after one or more services complete their work.

use_cases.title: Workflow Review
use_cases.description: Review an existing event-driven process for missing idempotency, timeout, correlation, and compensation behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Map a Simple Saga
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the saga-orchestration skill to map a saga for this workflow: [describe workflow]. Identify each step, success event, failure event, and compensation.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Orchestration or Choreography
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the saga-orchestration skill to compare orchestration and choreography for this process: [describe services and failure modes]. Recommend one approach and explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Design Compensation Logic
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the saga-orchestration skill to design compensating actions for these completed steps: [list steps]. Include idempotency rules and timeout behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Readiness
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the saga-orchestration skill to review this saga design for production readiness: [paste design]. Focus on correlation IDs, retries, timeouts, compensation ordering, and partial failure handling.

output_examples.input: Design a checkout saga for inventory, payment, shipping, and email confirmation.
output: A step sequence with reserve inventory, process payment, create shipment, and send confirmation.
A compensation plan with release reservation, refund payment, cancel shipment, and cancellation notice.
A failure model that explains which completed steps must be reversed for each failure point.

output_examples.input: Review whether my service workflow should use choreography.
output: A recommendation based on service coupling, workflow visibility, operational complexity, and failure handling.
A list of required events for each service and the compensating events for failure paths.
A short risk summary covering observability, duplicate events, and timeout handling.

output_examples.input: Add timeout handling to a long-running saga.
output: A timeout strategy for each saga step with clear ownership and retry limits.
A compensation trigger plan for expired steps.
Operational notes for logging, correlation IDs, and manual recovery.

best_practices: Define every compensation before implementing the forward action.
Make saga steps idempotent so retries and duplicate events are safe.
Use correlation IDs, durable state, and timeouts for every long-running step.
anti_patterns: Starting a saga without a tested rollback path for completed steps.
Sharing database transactions across services instead of using explicit messages.
Ignoring partial failures because each individual service appears reliable.

faq.question: What is this skill best used for?
faq.answer: It is best used for designing distributed workflows that need clear steps, failure handling, and compensating actions.

faq.question: Does it generate production-ready code?
faq.answer: It provides templates and patterns. Production use requires real persistence, messaging, observability, retries, and service-specific validation.

faq.question: Can it help choose between orchestration and choreography?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains both approaches and helps compare control, coupling, visibility, and operational complexity.

faq.question: Which languages does it target?
faq.answer: The examples use Python-style templates, but the saga design concepts apply to most backend languages.

faq.question: Does it handle payment or compliance rules?
faq.answer: No. It can structure workflow logic, but domain rules and compliance requirements need separate expert review.

faq.question: Can it review an existing saga design?
faq.answer: Yes. It can identify missing compensation, idempotency, timeout, correlation, and partial failure handling.
